title: A note on zero-density approaches for the difference between consecutive primes

thm:2.1.summary: Ingham's theorem (1937): a zero-free region $\sigma>1-A\tfrac{\log\log t}{\log t}$ for $\zeta$ gives the prime number theorem in short intervals $(x,x+x\exp(-c\sqrt{\log x})]$. The classical 'zero-free region' route, the weakest of the approaches surveyed.

thm:2.2.summary: Cramér (1920): under RH there is $c>0$ with $\pi(x+c\sqrt{x}\log x)-\pi(x)>\sqrt{x}$ for all $x\ge2$ — primes in intervals of length $\asymp\sqrt{x}\log x$. The conditional benchmark.

thm:2.3.summary: The classical zero-density route (Ingham, generalised): if $N(\sigma,T)\ll T^{b(1-\sigma)}\log^B T$ uniformly for $\tfrac12\le\sigma\le1$, then $(x,x+y]$ contains the expected number of primes for $y=x^\theta g(x)$ with $\theta>1-\tfrac1b$. The general theorem turning a zero-density exponent $b$ into a short-interval exponent $\theta$.

cor:2.4.summary: With the classical zero-density estimate ($b=\tfrac{12}{5}$, from Ingham/Huxley), thm:2.3 gives primes in intervals of length $x^{7/12+\epsilon}$ — Huxley's classical short-interval exponent.

cor:2.6.summary: Under the density hypothesis ($b=2$), thm:2.3 gives primes in intervals of length $x^{1/2+\epsilon}$ — the best the unweighted zero-density route can do.

lem:3.1.summary: The Heath-Brown–Iwaniec mean-value setup: for Dirichlet polynomials $M(s),N(s)$ of suitable lengths and a non-negative function $A(\sigma)$ with prescribed monotonicity, a weighted estimate for $\sum_\rho$ over zeros in the relevant box. The mechanism that lets a *weighted* zero-density estimate (better than the unweighted one in the relevant range) feed the short-interval argument.

cor:3.2.summary: The Heath-Brown–Iwaniec weighted zero-density estimate (1979): the explicit form of lem:3.1's bound, which underlies their result on primes in intervals of length $x^{0.55+\epsilon}$ — better than the $x^{7/12}$ from the unweighted route.

prop:3.3.summary: Sum over zeros of products of Dirichlet polynomials: $\sum_\rho|M(\rho)N(\rho)|$ bounded under the standard length/coefficient hypotheses. The estimate connecting the mean-value machinery to the explicit-formula sum.

lem:3.4.summary: A weaker-hypothesis variant of lem:3.1: the same conclusion assuming only $N(\sigma,T)\ll T^{A(\sigma)(1-\sigma)+o(1)}$ (an $o(1)$-exponent zero-density bound rather than a log-power one). Lets modern zero-density estimates be plugged in.

cor:3.5.summary: The Baker–Harman weighted estimate (Lemma 1(i) of Baker–Harman 1996): the conditions on the Dirichlet-polynomial lengths $M=x^u$, $N=x^v$, $T=x^{1-\theta-\epsilon/2}$ under which a weighted zero-density bound holds. Behind their $x^{0.535+\epsilon}$ short-interval result.

cor:3.6.summary: A refined parameter range for cor:3.5 (the $\tfrac19(26\cdots)$ conditions) — the optimised version of the Baker–Harman conditions used in the strongest classical short-interval bounds.

prop:4.1.summary: The explicit-formula / Perron identity $\sum_{x-y<mnr\le x}a_mb_n\Lambda(r)=y\sum_{\cdots}+\text{error}$, with the error controlled by the weighted sum-over-zeros estimates above. The bridge from 'few zeros (weighted)' to 'primes in $(x-y,x]$'.

edges.note: Ingham's theorem is the prototype; thm:2.3 is its quantitative zero-density-exponent form.
Plug $b=\tfrac{12}{5}$ (classical zero-density) into the general theorem.
The short-interval count comes from the explicit-formula identity with the error bounded by a zero-count.
The Heath-Brown–Iwaniec weighted estimate is the explicit form of the mean-value lemma.
The weighted zero-sum bound is built from the sum-over-zeros estimate for products of Dirichlet polynomials.
Same conclusion under an $o(1)$-exponent zero-density hypothesis instead of a log-power one — accommodates modern estimates.
Baker–Harman's weighted estimate is an instance of the weaker-hypothesis mean-value lemma.
Optimised parameter range for the Baker–Harman conditions.
The weighted zero-density estimate is the error bound in the explicit-formula identity — yielding primes in $x^{0.55+\epsilon}$ intervals.
Cramér's RH-conditional bound is the strong-hypothesis companion of the zero-free-region route.
Both the unweighted route and the weighted route ultimately count zeros via explicit-formula sums of (products of) Dirichlet polynomials.

classical_routes.description: The zero-free-region route (Ingham), the RH-conditional benchmark (Cramér), and the general zero-density-exponent ⟹ short-interval-exponent theorem with its corollaries.

weighted_zero_density.description: The Heath-Brown–Iwaniec and Baker–Harman weighted mean-value estimates that beat the unweighted route in the relevant range, in both their classical (log-power) and modern ($o(1)$-exponent) forms.

explicit_formula.description: The Perron/explicit-formula identity converting 'few zeros (weighted)' into 'primes in $(x-y,x]$'.

Ingham:1937.title: On the difference between consecutive primes
